county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1915 from Bonner County. Northernmost Idaho county, bordering Canada and Montana. Kootenai tribe homeland.
county_metadata.research_tips: Boundary County Recorder in Bonners Ferry holds deed records from 1915. Boundary County is Idaho's northernmost county, bordering British Columbia. The Kootenai Tribe of Idaho has its headquarters in Bonners Ferry — tribal records and BIA records at NARA Seattle document enrollment and allotment. Early settlement was linked to the Kootenai River valley gold mining and later to timber — Bonners Ferry Lumber Company records at the University of Idaho Library identify workers. Check Bonner County for pre-1915 records. The Boundary County Historical Society museum in Bonners Ferry has pioneer photographs and family files. Canadian border crossing records at NARA Pacific Alaska Region document families moving between British Columbia and northern Idaho. The Great Northern Railway (Bonners Ferry was a division point) brought settlers from the Upper Midwest.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1915 to present. County Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1915. County Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1947.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from parts of Bingham, Blaine, and Jefferson counties. Central Idaho county. Site of the first nuclear-generated electricity (1951).
county_metadata.research_tips: Butte County Recorder in Arco has deed records from 1917. Butte County is most famous as the site of the first nuclear-generated electricity in the world at Experimental Breeder Reactor I (EBR-I) in 1951, part of the Idaho National Laboratory complex. Ranch and farm families settled the Big Lost River valley before the nuclear era. Check Bingham, Blaine, and Jefferson counties for pre-1917 records. Craters of the Moon National Monument (1924) is within the county; NPS records at NARA document monument employees. BLM's GLO records document original homestead patents in the valley. The Butte County Historical Society in Arco has pioneer family files. Arco was also the first city lit by atomic power (1955). The extremely small population means county records, school censuses, and the Arco Advertiser newspaper provide near-complete family coverage.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1917 to present. County Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from Blaine County. Small high-desert county in south-central Idaho. Named for the camas lily, a food source for Native peoples.
county_metadata.research_tips: Camas County Recorder in Fairfield holds deed records from 1917. Camas County is one of Idaho's smallest and least-populated counties. The Camas Prairie attracted ranchers and farmers in the late 1800s; livestock brand records at ISHS identify early stockmen. Check Blaine County for pre-1917 records. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries in the county. The Camas County Historical Society in Fairfield preserves pioneer family photographs and records. Soldier Mountain and the surrounding hills attracted small-scale miners whose claims are documented at BLM. The Soldier Creek area was an early stopping point on the road between Boise and the Wood River mines. The extremely small population (consistently under 1,200) means that county records, school district records, and the Camas County Courier newspaper are particularly comprehensive for identifying all families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1892 from Ada County. Western Idaho agricultural county in the Boise Valley. Idaho's second most populous county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Canyon County Recorder in Caldwell has deed records from 1892. Canyon County is Idaho's agricultural heartland — irrigation from the Boise River transformed sagebrush desert into productive farmland. The Reclamation Act of 1902 and the Deer Flat Reservoir (Lake Lowell) brought thousands of homesteaders; Bureau of Reclamation project records at NARA Denver document water rights and settlers. Nampa and Caldwell grew as Oregon Short Line railroad towns. The Canyon County Historical Society museum in Nampa has pioneer family files. College of Idaho (founded 1891) in Caldwell holds early academic records. Japanese-American families farmed in Canyon County before WWII internment — Minidoka internment camp records at NARA document displaced families. Hispanic agricultural workers settled from the 1940s forward. Check Ada County for pre-1892 records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from parts of Bannock and Oneida counties. Southeastern Idaho county on the Oregon Trail. Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Caribou County Recorder in Soda Springs has deed records from 1919. Soda Springs was a famous landmark on the Oregon Trail — emigrants described the natural mineral springs in their diaries and letters. LDS ward records are the primary genealogical source for Caribou County families, with wards organized in the 1880s-1890s; many records are indexed on FamilySearch. The Oregon Trail ruts and landmarks in the county are documented in emigrant diaries held at multiple repositories. Check Bannock and Oneida counties for pre-1919 records. Phosphate mining became important in the 20th century — Monsanto and other mining company employment records identify workers. The Caribou County Historical Society in Soda Springs has pioneer family files and Oregon Trail artifacts. The Grace and Bancroft communities have distinct LDS settlement histories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1879 from Owyhee County. South-central Idaho county. City of Rocks (emigrant landmark). Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cassia County Recorder in Burley holds deed records from 1879. Cassia County includes the City of Rocks, a famous Oregon and California Trail landmark — emigrant signatures carved in stone and trail diaries document thousands of travelers. Mormon settlement began in the 1870s; LDS ward records for Albion, Declo, Burley, and other communities are primary genealogical sources indexed on FamilySearch. Albion State Normal School (now closed, founded 1893) records at ISHS document students and faculty. The Cassia County Historical Society museum in Burley has pioneer family files. Irrigation from the Milner Dam (1905) and Minidoka Project transformed the county into agricultural land; Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver document water rights holders. Japanese and Mexican laborers worked sugar beet fields. Check Owyhee County for pre-1879 records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Fremont County. Remote eastern Idaho county. Idaho's least-populated county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clark County Recorder in Dubois holds deed records from 1919. Clark County is Idaho's least-populated county (consistently under 1,000 residents) and one of the most remote. The high desert and mountain terrain supported sheep ranching — Basque sheepherders worked the ranges, and sheep company records at ISHS document this community. Check Fremont County for pre-1919 records. BLM's GLO records document the relatively few homestead entries in the county. The Birch Creek valley attracted early ranchers; livestock brand records at ISHS identify families. The extremely small population means that school district records, county commissioner minutes, and the local newspaper are particularly valuable for comprehensive family documentation. Small LDS communities in Dubois and Spencer have ward records on FamilySearch.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from Nez Perce County. North-central Idaho timber county along the Clearwater River. Lewis and Clark passed through in 1805.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clearwater County Recorder in Orofino has deed records from 1911. The Clearwater River valley was part of the Lewis and Clark route (1805-1806), and expedition journals reference the Nez Perce people who lived here. Timber dominated the economy — Potlatch Lumber Company (later Potlatch Corporation) and Clearwater Timber Company records at the University of Idaho Library Special Collections identify thousands of loggers and mill workers. The Clearwater Historical Museum in Orofino has pioneer family files and logging photographs. Dworshak Dam (1966-1972) displaced some families; Army Corps of Engineers records at NARA document affected property owners. Check Nez Perce County for pre-1911 records. Pierce City was Idaho's first gold discovery site (1860); mining district records at ISHS document early miners. The Nez Perce Reservation partially overlaps the county.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1881 from Alturas County (extinct). Central Idaho mountain county. Includes ghost towns of Bonanza and Custer from the 1870s gold rush.
county_metadata.research_tips: Custer County Recorder in Challis holds deed records from 1881. The Yankee Fork gold rush (1870s-1880s) created boomtowns at Bonanza and Custer, now ghost towns — mining district records and camp census rolls at ISHS document miners. The Custer Museum at Challis and the Yankee Fork Gold Dredge site preserve mining-era history. The Stanley Basin attracted ranchers and miners; early homestead records are at BLM's GLO database. The Land of the Yankee Fork State Park has interpretive records about mining families. Buster Keaton's dam scene and the Sawtooth Valley drew attention in later decades. Check Alturas County (extinct) records at ISHS for pre-1881 documentation. The extremely remote terrain means early records are thin; county records, mining claims, and the Challis Messenger newspaper are essential. The Salmon River drew Chinese placer miners whose tax records survive at ISHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1889 from Alturas County (extinct). South-central Idaho county. Home to Mountain Home Air Force Base.
county_metadata.research_tips: Elmore County Recorder in Mountain Home has deed records from 1889. Mountain Home grew as an Oregon Short Line railroad town on the high desert. Mountain Home Air Force Base (established 1943) has been the county's largest employer — military personnel records at NARA document servicemembers and civilian employees. Irrigation from the Anderson Ranch Dam (1950) and other projects brought farmers; Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver document water rights. Check Alturas County (extinct) records at ISHS for pre-1889 documentation. The Elmore County Historical Society museum in Mountain Home has pioneer family files. Atlanta and Rocky Bar were gold mining towns in the Boise Mountains; mining district records at ISHS document miners from the 1860s forward. Basque sheepherders worked the Elmore County ranges; sheep company records survive at ISHS and in private collections. The Mountain Home News newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS.
